examples.explanation: Start at (0,0) with value 1. Option 1: Move right to (0,1)=3. From (0,1): Option A: Move right to (0,2)=1, then down paths: (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives sum 1+3+1+1+1=7. Option B: Move down to (1,1)=5, then paths: right to (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+3+5+1+1=11; down to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+3+5+2+1=12. Min from (0,1) is 7. Option 2: Move down to (1,0)=1. From (1,0): Option A: Move right to (1,1)=5, then paths: right to (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+5+1+1=9; down to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+5+2+1=10. Option B: Move down to (2,0)=4, then right to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+4+2+1=9. Min from (1,0) is 9. Overall min is min(7,9)=7. Path: 1→3→1→1→1.

Start at (0,0)=1. Option 1: Move right to (0,1)=2. From (0,1): Option A: Move right to (0,2)=3, then down to (1,2)=6 gives 1+2+3+6=12. Option B: Move down to (1,1)=5, then right to (1,2)=6 gives 1+2+5+6=14. Min from (0,1) is 12. Option 2: Move down to (1,0)=4. From (1,0): Option A: Move right to (1,1)=5, then right to (1,2)=6 gives 1+4+5+6=16. Option B: Move right to (1,1)=5 via different path not applicable. Actually from (1,0), only right moves: to (1,1)=5 then (1,2)=6 gives 1+4+5+6=16. Min from (1,0) is 16. Overall min is min(12,16)=12. Path: 1→2→3→6.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(m+n))
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible paths from the top-left to bottom-right of the grid, summing values along each path and tracking the minimum. This approach is straightforward but highly inefficient due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Sum via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid to find minimum sum.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force recursion approach for the Minimum Path Sum problem. The intuition is straightforward: starting from the top-left cell (0,0), we can only move right or down to reach the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1). By recursively exploring both possible moves at each step, we generate all possible paths through the grid. For each path, we accumulate the sum of cell values along that path, and track the minimum sum found across all paths. The algorithm works because it systematically explores every possible route through the grid, ensuring we don't miss any potential minimum path. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define a recursive function minPath(i,j,currentSum) that computes the minimum path sum starting from cell (i,j) with currentSum accumulated so far. 2. Base cases: If we reach the bottom-right cell, return currentSum + grid[i][j]. If we go out of bounds, return infinity (or a very large number) to indicate an invalid path. 3. Recursive cases: For cell (i,j), we have two options: move right to (i,j+1) or move down to (i+1,j). Recursively compute the minimum path sum for both options and return the minimum of the two, adding grid[i][j] to the result. 4. Start the recursion from (0,0) with currentSum = 0. Time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) because at each cell (except boundary cells), we make two recursive calls, leading to exponential growth in the recursion tree. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the recursion call stack depth, which equals the path length. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle out-of-bounds conditions properly, incorrectly accumulating the sum (e.g., adding grid values multiple times), and not using a large enough value for infinity in base cases. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def minPathSum(grid): m,n = len(grid), len(grid[0]); def dfs(i,j): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return grid[i][j]; if i>=m or j>=n: return float('inf'); return grid[i][j] + min(dfs(i+1,j), dfs(i,j+1)); return dfs(0,0).

examples.trace: Start at (0,0) with value 1. Option 1: Move right to (0,1)=3. From (0,1): Option A: Move right to (0,2)=1, then down paths: (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives sum 1+3+1+1+1=7. Option B: Move down to (1,1)=5, then paths: right to (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+3+5+1+1=11; down to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+3+5+2+1=12. Min from (0,1) is 7. Option 2: Move down to (1,0)=1. From (1,0): Option A: Move right to (1,1)=5, then paths: right to (1,2)=1 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+5+1+1=9; down to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+5+2+1=10. Option B: Move down to (2,0)=4, then right to (2,1)=2 to (2,2)=1 gives 1+1+4+2+1=9. Min from (1,0) is 9. Overall min is min(7,9)=7. Path: 1→3→1→1→1.

Start at (0,0)=1. Option 1: Move right to (0,1)=2. From (0,1): Option A: Move right to (0,2)=3, then down to (1,2)=6 gives 1+2+3+6=12. Option B: Move down to (1,1)=5, then right to (1,2)=6 gives 1+2+5+6=14. Min from (0,1) is 12. Option 2: Move down to (1,0)=4. From (1,0): Option A: Move right to (1,1)=5, then right to (1,2)=6 gives 1+4+5+6=16. Option B: Move right to (1,1)=5 via different path not applicable. Actually from (1,0), only right moves: to (1,1)=5 then (1,2)=6 gives 1+4+5+6=16. Min from (1,0) is 16. Overall min is min(12,16)=12. Path: 1→2→3→6.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Path Exploration Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that explores all paths from a given cell to the destination.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that, given a current cell position (i,j), computes the minimum path sum from that cell to the bottom-right corner of the grid. The intuition is that at any cell, the minimum path to the destination is the value of the current cell plus the minimum of the paths from the cell to the right and the cell below. This recursive decomposition breaks the problem into smaller subproblems. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Function signature: dfs(i,j) returns the minimum path sum from (i,j) to (m-1,n-1). 2. Base cases: If (i,j) is the destination cell (m-1,n-1), return grid[i][j]. If i or j is out of bounds (i>=m or j>=n), return infinity to indicate an invalid path. 3. Recursive case: Return grid[i][j] + min(dfs(i+1,j), dfs(i,j+1)). This computes the sum by adding the current cell's value to the minimum of the paths from the two possible next moves. Time complexity for this function alone, when called from a specific (i,j), is O(2^(m+n-i-j)) in the worst case, as it explores all paths from that point. Space complexity is O(m+n-i-j) for the recursion depth. This sub-routine connects to the full problem by being the core recursive engine; the full solution simply calls dfs(0,0). Common mistakes include: incorrect base case conditions (e.g., missing out-of-bounds check), not using a large enough value for infinity (causing min() to pick invalid paths), and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def dfs(i,j): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return grid[i][j]; if i>=m or j>=n: return float('inf'); return grid[i][j] + min(dfs(i+1,j), dfs(i,j+1)).

examples.trace: dfs(0,0): current cell value 1. Option A: dfs(1,0) for down move. dfs(1,0): value 3, at (1,0). Option A1: dfs(2,0) out of bounds returns inf. Option A2: dfs(1,1): value 4, at destination, returns 4. So dfs(1,0) returns 3 + min(inf,4)=7. Option B: dfs(0,1) for right move. dfs(0,1): value 2. Option B1: dfs(1,1): returns 4. Option B2: dfs(0,2) out of bounds returns inf. So dfs(0,1) returns 2 + min(4,inf)=6. dfs(0,0) returns 1 + min(7,6)=7.
dfs(0,0): at destination (0,0) since grid is 1x1, so base case returns grid[0][0]=5 directly.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition of Path Choices
ladders.desc: Understand how the problem breaks into subproblems via right/down moves at each cell.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the fundamental insight of the brute force approach: at any cell in the grid, the minimum path sum to the destination can be expressed recursively as the current cell's value plus the minimum of the paths from the two adjacent cells (right and down). The intuition is based on the constraint that we can only move right or down, so from any cell (i,j), the optimal path to the bottom-right must go through either (i+1,j) or (i,j+1). Therefore, if we know the minimum path sums from those two cells, we can compute the minimum from (i,j) by adding grid[i][j] and taking the min. This is a recursive decomposition that reduces the problem to smaller instances. The step-by-step logic: 1. Let minPath(i,j) be the minimum sum from (i,j) to (m-1,n-1). 2. Then minPath(i,j) = grid[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i,j+1)), for i<m-1 and j<n-1. 3. Boundary cases: At the destination, minPath(m-1,n-1) = grid[m-1][n-1]. If i or j goes out of bounds, treat it as an invalid path with infinite cost. This logic is applied recursively starting from (0,0). Time analysis: This leads to overlapping subproblems, but in brute force, we recompute them, giving exponential time. Space is linear due to recursion depth. This connects to the key sub-routine as it defines the recurrence relation that the dfs function implements. Common mistakes: Misunderstanding the direction (should be min of future paths, not past), incorrect handling of grid boundaries, and not considering the current cell's value in the sum. Real code pattern: The recurrence is embodied in the return statement: return grid[i][j] + min(dfs(i+1,j), dfs(i,j+1)).

examples.trace: Compute minPath(1,0): grid[1][0]=3 + min(minPath(2,0), minPath(1,1)). minPath(2,0) is out of bounds = inf. minPath(1,1)=4. So minPath(1,0)=3+min(inf,4)=7. Compute minPath(0,1): grid[0][1]=2 + min(minPath(1,1), minPath(0,2)). minPath(1,1)=4, minPath(0,2) out of bounds=inf. So minPath(0,1)=2+min(4,inf)=6. Then minPath(0,0)=1+min(7,6)=7.

minPath(2,1): grid[2][1]=2 + min(minPath(3,1), minPath(2,2)). minPath(3,1) out of bounds=inf, minPath(2,2)=1. So minPath(2,1)=2+min(inf,1)=3. minPath(1,2): grid[1][2]=1 + min(minPath(2,2), minPath(1,3)). minPath(2,2)=1, minPath(1,3) out of bounds=inf. So minPath(1,2)=1+min(1,inf)=2. Then minPath(1,1)=5+min(3,2)=7.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with Recursion
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse a 2D grid recursively, exploring multiple paths from a start point.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the skill of recursively traversing a 2D grid, which is essential for the brute force approach. The intuition is that a grid can be navigated by making recursive calls to adjacent cells, allowing us to explore all possible routes. In this context, we restrict moves to right and down to reach from top-left to bottom-right. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define a recursive function traverse(i,j) that visits cell (i,j). 2. Base case: If (i,j) is the destination (m-1,n-1), we have completed a path. 3. Recursive cases: If i < m-1, make a recursive call to traverse(i+1,j) to move down. If j < n-1, make a recursive call to traverse(i,j+1) to move right. 4. This generates all possible paths by exploring both options at each step where available. Time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) in the worst case, as each cell (except boundaries) leads to two recursive calls, creating a binary recursion tree. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the maximum recursion depth. This connects to the core logic by providing the traversal mechanism that underpins the path exploration; the core logic adds the sum accumulation on top of this traversal. Common mistakes include: infinite recursion due to missing base cases, incorrect condition checks for moves (e.g., using <= instead of <), and not handling grid bounds properly. Real code pattern: def traverse(i,j): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return; if i+1 < m: traverse(i+1,j); if j+1 < n: traverse(i,j+1).

examples.trace: traverse(0,0): not at destination (1,1). Option 1: i+1=1 < 2, so traverse(1,0). traverse(1,0): not at dest. Option A: i+1=2 not <2, skip. Option B: j+1=1 <2, so traverse(1,1). traverse(1,1): at dest, return. Back to traverse(0,0). Option 2: j+1=1 <2, so traverse(0,1). traverse(0,1): not at dest. Option A: i+1=1 <2, so traverse(1,1). traverse(1,1): at dest, return. Option B: j+1=2 not <2, skip. Paths: (0,0)→(1,0)→(1,1) and (0,0)→(0,1)→(1,1).

traverse(0,0): dest is (0,2). Option 1: i+1=1 not <1 (since m=1), skip. Option 2: j+1=1 <3, so traverse(0,1). traverse(0,1): not at dest. Option A: i+1=1 not <1, skip. Option B: j+1=2 <3, so traverse(0,2). traverse(0,2): at dest, return. Path: (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implement a simple recursive function that uses base cases to stop recursion.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves writing a recursive function that includes proper base cases to terminate the recursion, a fundamental skill for the brute force approach. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by breaking them into smaller subproblems, but it must have stopping conditions to avoid infinite loops. In this context, we practice with a simple task: counting the number of paths from a start cell to a destination in a grid, but only moving right or down. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Define a function countPaths(i,j) that returns the number of ways to reach (m-1,n-1) from (i,j). 2. Base cases: If (i,j) is the destination, return 1 (one way to stay there). If i or j is out of bounds (i>=m or j>=n), return 0 (no valid path). 3. Recursive case: Return countPaths(i+1,j) + countPaths(i,j+1), summing the paths from moving down and right. Time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) due to exponential recursive calls. Space complexity is O(m+n) for recursion depth. This connects to grid traversal as it uses the same recursive structure but for counting instead of traversing; it reinforces the idea of base cases and recursive decomposition. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases leading to stack overflow, incorrect addition in recursive case, and misindexing. Real code pattern: def countPaths(i,j): if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return 1; if i>=m or j>=n: return 0; return countPaths(i+1,j) + countPaths(i,j+1).

examples.trace: countPaths(0,0): not at dest. Option A: countPaths(1,0). countPaths(1,0): not at dest. Option A1: countPaths(2,0) out of bounds returns 0. Option A2: countPaths(1,1): at dest returns 1. So countPaths(1,0)=0+1=1. Option B: countPaths(0,1). countPaths(0,1): not at dest. Option B1: countPaths(1,1) returns 1. Option B2: countPaths(0,2) out of bounds returns 0. So countPaths(0,1)=1+0=1. countPaths(0,0)=1+1=2.

countPaths(0,0): dest is (0,2). Option A: countPaths(1,0) out of bounds returns 0. Option B: countPaths(0,1). countPaths(0,1): not at dest. Option B1: countPaths(1,1) out of bounds returns 0. Option B2: countPaths(0,2): at dest returns 1. So countPaths(0,1)=0+1=1. countPaths(0,0)=0+1=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of recursion: a function that calls itself with a stopping condition.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces recursion, a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. The intuition is that many problems can be broken down into similar subproblems; recursion provides an elegant way to express this. A recursive function must have: 1. Base case(s): Simple condition(s) where the function returns a value without further recursion, preventing infinite loops. 2. Recursive case(s): Where the function calls itself with modified parameters, moving toward the base case. For beginners, we start with a simple example: calculating factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1), with base case factorial(0)=1. The step-by-step algorithm for factorial: 1. Define function factorial(n). 2. Base case: if n == 0, return 1. 3. Recursive case: return n * factorial(n-1). Time complexity is O(n) as it makes n recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This connects to basic operation as it teaches the core pattern of recursion that will be extended to grid problems; understanding base cases is critical for avoiding errors in more complex recursions. Common mistakes: Missing base case causing infinite recursion, incorrect recursive step (e.g., not reducing n), and not understanding the call stack. Real code pattern: def factorial(n): if n == 0: return 1; return n * factorial(n-1).

examples.trace: factorial(3): n=3 not 0, so recursive case: return 3 * factorial(2). Call factorial(2): n=2 not 0, return 2 * factorial(1). Call factorial(1): n=1 not 0, return 1 * factorial(0). Call factorial(0): n=0, base case returns 1. Back to factorial(1): 1 * 1 = 1. Back to factorial(2): 2 * 1 = 2. Back to factorial(3): 3 * 2 = 6.
factorial(0): n=0, base case returns 1 directly.

approaches.name: Top-Down Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store the minimum path sum for each cell, avoiding redundant calculations by caching results. This approach reduces time complexity significantly compared to brute force while maintaining a clear recursive structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Sum with Top-Down DP and Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding minimum path sum from top-left to bottom-right in a grid using recursion with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum sum of numbers along a path from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1) of a grid, moving only right or down. The top-down DP approach with memoization solves this by breaking it into overlapping subproblems: the minimum path sum to reach cell (i,j) depends on the minimum sums to reach cells (i-1,j) and (i,j-1). We use recursion to explore these dependencies, but instead of recomputing values repeatedly, we cache results in a memo dictionary. The intuition is that many paths share common subpaths, so storing intermediate results eliminates exponential recomputation. The algorithm defines a recursive function minPath(i,j) that returns the minimum sum from (i,j) to the destination. Base cases handle boundaries: if out of bounds, return infinity (invalid path); if at destination, return grid value. Otherwise, compute grid[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i,j+1)), caching results. Time complexity is O(m*n) as each cell is computed once, and space is O(m*n) for memo storage plus recursion stack. This approach maintains clarity of the recursive relation while being efficient. Common mistakes include not handling boundaries correctly, forgetting to add current cell value, or using incorrect memo keys. The connection to simpler levels is that this integrates all foundational concepts: grid traversal, recursion, memoization, and DP principles.

examples.trace:
Start at (0,0): minPath(0,0). Compute minPath(1,0) and minPath(0,1).
- minPath(1,0): grid[1][0]=1 + min(minPath(2,0), minPath(1,1)). minPath(2,0): grid[2][0]=4 + min(minPath(3,0), minPath(2,1)). minPath(3,0): out of bounds → ∞. minPath(2,1): grid[2][1]=2 + min(minPath(3,1), minPath(2,2)). minPath(3,1): ∞. minPath(2,2): grid[2][2]=1 + min(minPath(3,2), minPath(2,3)). Both ∞ → 1. So minPath(2,1)=2+1=3. minPath(2,0)=4+min(∞,3)=7. minPath(1,1): grid[1][1]=5 + min(minPath(2,1), minPath(1,2)). minPath(2,1)=3 (cached). minPath(1,2): grid[1][2]=1 + min(minPath(2,2), minPath(1,3)). minPath(2,2)=1, minPath(1,3)=∞ → 1+1=2. So minPath(1,1)=5+min(3,2)=7. Thus minPath(1,0)=1+min(7,7)=8.
- minPath(0,1): grid[0][1]=3 + min(minPath(1,1), minPath(0,2)). minPath(1,1)=7 (cached). minPath(0,2): grid[0][2]=1 + min(minPath(1,2), minPath(0,3)). minPath(1,2)=2, minPath(0,3)=∞ → 1+2=3. So minPath(0,1)=3+min(7,3)=6.
Thus minPath(0,0)=1+min(8,6)=7. Path: 1→3→1→1→1 (sum 7).

Start at (0,0): minPath(0,0). Compute minPath(1,0) and minPath(0,1).
- minPath(1,0): grid[1][0]=4 + min(minPath(2,0), minPath(1,1)). minPath(2,0): ∞. minPath(1,1): grid[1][1]=5 + min(minPath(2,1), minPath(1,2)). minPath(2,1): ∞. minPath(1,2): grid[1][2]=6 + min(minPath(2,2), minPath(1,3)). Both ∞ → 6. So minPath(1,1)=5+6=11. Thus minPath(1,0)=4+min(∞,11)=15.
- minPath(0,1): grid[0][1]=2 + min(minPath(1,1), minPath(0,2)). minPath(1,1)=11 (cached). minPath(0,2): grid[0][2]=3 + min(minPath(1,2), minPath(0,3)). minPath(1,2)=6, minPath(0,3)=∞ → 3+6=9. So minPath(0,1)=2+min(11,9)=11.
Thus minPath(0,0)=1+min(15,11)=12. Path: 1→2→3→6 (sum 12).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization for Cell Minimum Path
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive helper function that computes minimum path sum from a given cell to destination with caching.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the top-down DP approach. It defines a function minPath(i,j) that returns the minimum sum from cell (i,j) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1). The intuition is dynamic programming: the solution for a cell depends on solutions for cells to its right and down, forming a recurrence relation. The algorithm uses recursion to compute these dependencies, but to avoid exponential time, it caches results in a memo dictionary keyed by (i,j). Steps: 1. Check if (i,j) is out of bounds; if so, return a large value (e.g., infinity) to indicate an invalid path. 2. If at destination (i==m-1 and j==n-1), return grid[i][j]. 3. Check memo for (i,j); if present, return cached value. 4. Otherwise, compute value = grid[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i,j+1)). 5. Store value in memo and return it. Time complexity for this function on a single call is O(1) plus recursive calls, but with memoization, overall it contributes to O(m*n) total. Space is O(m*n) for memo. Connection to L0: this function is called recursively from the top-left cell to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (e.g., not handling out-of-bounds), forgetting to add current cell value, or using mutable default arguments for memo. The pseudocode pattern: def minPath(i,j, memo={}): if (i,j) in memo: return memo[(i,j)]; if i>=m or j>=n: return float('inf'); if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return grid[i][j]; down = minPath(i+1,j, memo); right = minPath(i,j+1, memo); result = grid[i][j] + min(down, right); memo[(i,j)] = result; return result.

examples.trace:
Call minPath(0,0). Not in memo, not out of bounds, not at destination. Compute minPath(1,0) and minPath(0,1).
- minPath(1,0): grid[1][0]=3. Not in memo, not out of bounds, not at destination. Compute minPath(2,0) and minPath(1,1). minPath(2,0): out of bounds → ∞. minPath(1,1): grid[1][1]=4, at destination → 4. So minPath(1,0)=3+min(∞,4)=7. Store memo[(1,0)]=7.
- minPath(0,1): grid[0][1]=2. Not in memo, not out of bounds, not at destination. Compute minPath(1,1) and minPath(0,2). minPath(1,1)=4 (cached). minPath(0,2): out of bounds → ∞. So minPath(0,1)=2+min(4,∞)=6. Store memo[(0,1)]=6.
Thus minPath(0,0)=1+min(7,6)=7. Store memo[(0,0)]=7.
Call minPath(0,0). Not in memo, not out of bounds. Check destination: i=0, j=0 and grid is 1x1, so at destination. Return grid[0][0]=5. Store memo[(0,0)]=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Minimum Path Sum
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence relation: minPath(i,j) = grid[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i,j+1)).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight of the DP solution: the recurrence relation that defines the minimum path sum from any cell (i,j) to the destination. The intuition is that to reach the bottom-right corner from (i,j), you must move either down to (i+1,j) or right to (i,j+1). Therefore, the minimum sum from (i,j) is the value at (i,j) plus the minimum of the sums from the two possible next cells. This relation breaks the problem into smaller overlapping subproblems, enabling efficient computation via DP. The algorithm involves defining this relation mathematically and using it to compute values iteratively or recursively. Steps: 1. Identify base cases: if at destination, minPath = grid value; if out of bounds, treat as infinite cost. 2. For other cells, apply the recurrence. This relation is valid because moving only right or down ensures no cycles, and the optimal solution exhibits optimal substructure. Time and space analysis depend on implementation, but the relation itself is O(1) per cell. Connection to L1: this relation is implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes include incorrect direction (e.g., using up or left), not adding current cell value, or misapplying min to non-existent cells. The pseudocode pattern: if i==m-1 and j==n-1: return grid[i][j]; else: return grid[i][j] + min(minPath(i+1,j), minPath(i,j+1)), with proper boundary checks. This logic is foundational for both top-down and bottom-up DP approaches.

examples.trace:
Compute minPath(1,0): at cell (1,0)=3, apply recurrence: 3 + min(minPath(2,0), minPath(1,1)). minPath(2,0): out of bounds → ∞. minPath(1,1): destination → 4. So minPath(1,0)=3+min(∞,4)=7.
Compute minPath(0,1): at cell (0,1)=2, apply: 2 + min(minPath(1,1), minPath(0,2)). minPath(1,1)=4, minPath(0,2): ∞ → 2+min(4,∞)=6.
Thus minPath(0,0)=1+min(7,6)=7.

Compute minPath(1,1): destination → 8.
Compute minPath(0,2): out of bounds → ∞.
Thus minPath(0,1)=6+min(8,∞)=14.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization (Caching) for Recursive Functions
ladders.desc: Implement memoization to store and reuse results of expensive recursive calls, avoiding recomputation.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is an optimization technique used in top-down dynamic programming to store the results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. The intuition is that many recursive problems, like minimum path sum, have overlapping subproblems where the same subproblem is solved multiple times. By caching results, we reduce time complexity from exponential to polynomial. The algorithm involves: 1. Define a memo data structure (e.g., dictionary or 2D array) to store results keyed by function parameters. 2. At the start of the recursive function, check if the result for the current parameters is already in memo; if so, return it. 3. Otherwise, compute the result, store it in memo, and return it. For the path sum problem, we key by grid coordinates (i,j). Time analysis: without memoization, recursion could be O(2^(m+n)); with memoization, each cell is computed once, so O(m*n). Space is O(m*n) for memo storage. Connection to L2: memoization is applied to the recurrence relation to make it efficient. Common mistakes include using mutable default arguments incorrectly in Python, not checking memo before computation, or caching incorrect values. The pseudocode pattern: memo = {}; def minPath(i,j): if (i,j) in memo: return memo[(i,j)]; result = compute value; memo[(i,j)] = result; return result. This building block is essential for any top-down DP solution.

examples.input: Function calls: minPath(0,0), minPath(0,1), minPath(0,0) again
examples.trace:
First call minPath(0,0): not in memo, compute result=7, store memo[(0,0)]=7.
Call minPath(0,1): not in memo, compute result=6, store memo[(0,1)]=6.
Second call minPath(0,0): found in memo, return 7 without recomputation.

examples.input: Function calls: minPath(1,1), minPath(1,0), minPath(0,1), minPath(1,1) again
examples.trace:
Call minPath(1,1): compute and store.
Call minPath(1,0): may call minPath(1,1), which is now cached.
Call minPath(0,1): may call minPath(1,1) and minPath(0,2), using cached if available.
Second call minPath(1,1): return cached value.

testCases.input: memo={}, call minPath(2,3) where out of bounds
memo={}, call minPath(0,0) and minPath(0,1) sharing subcalls
memo={}, call minPath(1,2) then minPath(1,1) then minPath(1,2)
memo={}, call minPath(0,0) and minPath(1,0) for grid 2x2
memo={}, call minPath(0,0) multiple times in loop

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal with Recursion (Moving Right/Down)
ladders.desc: Use recursion to traverse a grid from a starting cell to a destination, moving only right or down.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental operation of recursively exploring paths in a grid with restricted movements. The intuition is to simulate all possible paths from a starting cell to a destination by making recursive calls for each allowed move. Since we can only move right (increase column) or down (increase row), this ensures no cycles and a finite number of paths. The algorithm: define a recursive function traverse(i,j) that represents being at cell (i,j). Base cases: if out of bounds, return (invalid); if at destination, return (success). Otherwise, recursively call traverse(i+1,j) for down and traverse(i,j+1) for right. This operation doesn't compute sums yet but establishes the traversal pattern. Time complexity without memoization is exponential O(2^(m+n)) due to exploring all paths; space is O(m+n) for recursion depth. Connection to L3: this traversal is the skeleton on which memoization and sum computation are added. Common mistakes include incorrect base conditions, moving in wrong directions, or infinite recursion from cycles (not an issue here due to right/down only). The pseudocode pattern: def traverse(i,j): if i>=m or j>=n: return; if i==m-1 and j==n-1: process destination; else: traverse(i+1,j); traverse(i,j+1). This operation is key for many grid-based DP problems.

examples.trace: Call traverse(0,0). Not at dest, not out of bounds. Call traverse(1,0): not at dest, call traverse(2,0): out of bounds return; call traverse(1,1): at dest, process. Return to traverse(1,0), then return. Call traverse(0,1): not at dest, call traverse(1,1): at dest, process; call traverse(0,2): out of bounds. Return.
Call traverse(0,0). Not at dest. Call traverse(1,0): out of bounds return. Call traverse(0,1): not at dest, call traverse(1,1): out of bounds; call traverse(0,2): at dest, process. Return.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recursion Basics: Function Calls Itself
ladders.desc: Understand recursion as a function that calls itself with smaller inputs to solve problems.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. The intuition is to break down a complex problem into simpler, similar subproblems until a base case is reached that can be solved directly. For the minimum path sum, recursion allows us to express the solution in terms of solutions for adjacent cells. The algorithm involves: 1. Define a recursive function with parameters that represent the current state. 2. Identify base cases: simple scenarios where the result is known without further recursion (e.g., reaching destination or out of bounds). 3. In the recursive case, make one or more recursive calls with modified parameters (e.g., moving to next cell). 4. Combine results from recursive calls if needed. Time and space analysis depend on the problem; recursion can lead to exponential time without optimization, but it provides a clear, intuitive solution structure. Connection to L4: recursion is used to traverse the grid. Common mistakes include missing base cases (leading to infinite recursion), not reducing problem size (causing stack overflow), or incorrect combination of results. The pseudocode pattern: def recursive_func(param): if base_case: return base_value; else: smaller_param = modify(param); return combine(recursive_func(smaller_param)). This foundation is essential for many DSA concepts, including DP, tree traversal, and divide-and-conquer.

examples.input: Compute factorial of n using recursion
examples.trace: factorial(3): not base case (n>1), call factorial(2). factorial(2): call factorial(1). factorial(1): base case return 1. Return to factorial(2): 2*1=2. Return to factorial(3): 3*2=6.

examples.input: Count steps to reduce number to 1: if even, divide by 2; if odd, subtract 1.
examples.trace: steps(5): odd, call steps(4). steps(4): even, call steps(2). steps(2): even, call steps(1). steps(1): base case return 0. Return to steps(2): 1+0=1. Return to steps(4): 1+1=2. Return to steps(5): 1+2=3.

testCases.input: sum of first n natural numbers, n=3

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Iteratively compute the minimum path sum for each cell using a 2D DP array, starting from the top-left and filling based on values from the left and top cells. This method avoids recursion overhead and builds the solution incrementally.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Sum with Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding minimum path sum in a grid using 2D DP array.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to the Minimum Path Sum problem using bottom-up dynamic programming with a 2D array. The intuition is that for any cell (i,j), the minimum path sum to reach it is the value at that cell plus the minimum of the path sums to reach the cell above (i-1,j) or to the left (i,j-1). This works because you can only move right or down, so any path to (i,j) must come from one of those two directions. We build a DP table where dp[i][j] represents the minimum path sum to reach cell (i,j). We initialize dp[0][0] = grid[0][0], then fill the first row and first column (since those cells only have one possible incoming direction), then fill the rest of the table row by row. The final answer is at dp[m-1][n-1]. Time complexity is O(m*n) as we visit each cell once. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP table. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete DP table filling logic. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (1x1 grid), incorrect initialization of first row/column, or using the wrong recurrence relation. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with same dimensions as grid, set dp[0][0] = grid[0][0], fill first row: dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] + grid[0][j], fill first column: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] + grid[i][0], then for i in 1..m-1, j in 1..n-1: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) + grid[i][j], return dp[m-1][n-1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[2][3]. dp[0][0] = 1. Step 2: Fill first row: dp[0][1] = 1+2=3, dp[0][2] = 3+3=6. Step 3: Fill first column: dp[1][0] = 1+4=5. Step 4: Fill remaining cells: dp[1][1] = min(dp[0][1], dp[1][0]) + 5 = min(3,5)+5=8, dp[1][2] = min(dp[0][2], dp[1][1]) + 6 = min(6,8)+6=12. Final answer: dp[1][2] = 12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Filling the DP Table
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP table filling logic for minimum path sum.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the main DP table filling algorithm, which is the heart of the solution. The intuition is that we can compute the minimum path sum to each cell based on previously computed values. We create a DP table dp where dp[i][j] = minimum path sum to reach cell (i,j). The recurrence relation is: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) for i>0 and j>0. For the first row (i=0, j>0), we can only come from the left: dp[0][j] = grid[0][j] + dp[0][j-1]. For the first column (j=0, i>0), we can only come from above: dp[i][0] = grid[i][0] + dp[i-1][0]. We initialize dp[0][0] = grid[0][0]. The algorithm: 1. Get dimensions m,n. 2. Create dp array size m x n. 3. Set dp[0][0] = grid[0][0]. 4. Fill first row: for j=1 to n-1: dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] + grid[0][j]. 5. Fill first column: for i=1 to m-1: dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] + grid[i][0]. 6. Fill rest: for i=1 to m-1, for j=1 to n-1: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) + grid[i][j]. 7. Return dp[m-1][n-1]. Time: O(m*n), Space: O(m*n). This connects to L0 as the complete implementation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loops, forgetting to initialize dp[0][0], incorrect handling of 1x1 grid. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(m)], dp[0][0]=grid[0][0], for j in range(1,n): dp[0][j]=dp[0][j-1]+grid[0][j], for i in range(1,m): dp[i][0]=dp[i-1][0]+grid[i][0], for i in range(1,m): for j in range(1,n): dp[i][j]=min(dp[i-1][j],dp[i][j-1])+grid[i][j], return dp[m-1][n-1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[2][2]. dp[0][0] = grid[0][0] = 1. Step 2: Fill first row: dp[0][1] = dp[0][0] + grid[0][1] = 1+2=3. Step 3: Fill first column: dp[1][0] = dp[0][0] + grid[1][0] = 1+3=4. Step 4: Fill dp[1][1] = min(dp[0][1], dp[1][0]) + grid[1][1] = min(3,4)+4=7. Final dp table: [[1,3],[4,7]].
Step 1: Initialize dp[1][1]. dp[0][0] = grid[0][0] = 5. No first row or column to fill (since m=1,n=1). No remaining cells. Final dp table: [[5]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence Relation
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recurrence relation that drives the DP solution. The intuition is optimal substructure: the minimum path to cell (i,j) consists of the minimum path to either the cell above or left, plus the current cell's value. Since you can only move right or down, to reach (i,j), you must come from (i-1,j) (above) or (i,j-1) (left). Therefore, the minimum path sum to (i,j) is the minimum of the paths to those two predecessor cells, plus grid[i][j]. This recurrence works for all cells except those on the top row or left column, which have only one predecessor. The algorithm for applying this relation: Given dp values for (i-1,j) and (i,j-1), compute dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). This must be applied in an order where dp[i-1][j] and dp[i][j-1] are already computed (typically row-by-row, left-to-right). Time for computing one cell is O(1). Space for storing dp values is O(m*n). This connects to L1 as the key computation step in filling the DP table. Common mistakes: Using max instead of min, adding grid[i][j] before taking min, incorrect order of computation. Real code pattern: For a given i,j: if i>0 and j>0: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) elif i==0 and j>0: dp[0][j] = grid[0][j] + dp[0][j-1] elif j==0 and i>0: dp[i][0] = grid[i][0] + dp[i-1][0] else: dp[0][0] = grid[0][0].

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify cell (1,1) with grid value 5. Step 2: Get dp values from above (0,1)=4 and left (1,0)=2. Step 3: Apply recurrence: dp[1][1] = grid[1][1] + min(dp[0][1], dp[1][0]) = 5 + min(4,2) = 5+2 = 7.

examples.input: grid[0][2]=3, dp[0][1]=5, compute dp[0][2] (first row case)
examples.trace: Step 1: Cell (0,2) is in first row (i=0). Step 2: Since i=0, only left neighbor exists at (0,1) with dp=5. Step 3: Apply first row rule: dp[0][2] = grid[0][2] + dp[0][1] = 3 + 5 = 8.

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D Array Traversal and Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Traverse a 2D grid and access adjacent cells (above and left).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a 2D array in a specific order and accessing neighboring cells. The intuition is that to compute values for each cell, we need to access values from the cell above (i-1,j) and left (i,j-1). This requires careful traversal order: typically row-by-row from top to bottom, and within each row left-to-right, ensuring that when we compute dp[i][j], both dp[i-1][j] (above) and dp[i][j-1] (left) have already been computed. The algorithm: 1. Get dimensions m,n. 2. For i from 0 to m-1: For j from 0 to n-1: Access grid[i][j]. 3. To access above cell: if i>0: grid[i-1][j]. 4. To access left cell: if j>0: grid[i][j-1]. We must handle edge cases where i=0 (no above cell) or j=0 (no left cell). Time: O(m*n) for full traversal. Space: O(1) extra. This connects to L2 as it provides the mechanism to access the dp values needed for the recurrence relation. Common mistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds indices (i-1 when i=0, j-1 when j=0), incorrect traversal order (e.g., column-major), not checking bounds. Real code pattern: m,n = len(grid), len(grid[0]); for i in range(m): for j in range(n): current = grid[i][j]; if i>0: above = grid[i-1][j]; if j>0: left = grid[i][j-1]; else handle edge case.

examples.output: Traversal order: (0,0): val=1, no above/left; (0,1): val=2, above=None, left=1; (1,0): val=3, above=1, left=None; (1,1): val=4, above=2, left=3
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0,j=0: current=1, i=0 so no above, j=0 so no left. Step 2: i=0,j=1: current=2, i=0 so no above, j>0 so left=grid[0][0]=1. Step 3: i=1,j=0: current=3, i>0 so above=grid[0][0]=1, j=0 so no left. Step 4: i=1,j=1: current=4, i>0 so above=grid[0][1]=2, j>0 so left=grid[1][0]=3.

examples.output: Traversal order: (0,0): val=5, no above/left; (0,1): val=6, above=None, left=5
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0,j=0: current=5, no above/left. Step 2: i=0,j=1: current=6, no above (i=0), left=grid[0][0]=5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min of Two Numbers and Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Compute minimum of two values and access 2D array elements.
ladders.explanation: This level covers two fundamental operations: finding the minimum of two numbers and accessing elements in a 2D array. The intuition is simple: we need to compare two path sums to choose the smaller one, and we need to retrieve values from specific positions in the grid. For min operation: given two numbers a and b, min(a,b) returns the smaller value. This is O(1) time. For 2D array indexing: a 2D array grid with m rows and n columns is typically represented as a list of lists in Python. grid[i][j] accesses the element at row i, column j (0-indexed). Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 ≤ i < m, 0 ≤ j < n. Time: O(1) for both operations. Space: O(1). This connects to L3 as these operations are used when accessing neighbors and applying the recurrence. Common mistakes: Using 1-indexing instead of 0-indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, incorrect min implementation (e.g., using max). Real code pattern: For min: smaller = a if a < b else b. For indexing: value = grid[i][j]; check if 0 <= i < len(grid) and 0 <= j < len(grid[0]). Practice with small grids to build intuition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 5 and 3. Step 2: Since 3 < 5, min(5,3) = 3.
Step 1: grid has 2 rows (0,1) and 2 columns (0,1). Step 2: grid[1][0] means row index 1, column index 0. Step 3: Row 1 is [3,4], column 0 gives 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Arrays and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand 2D arrays (matrices) and perform addition/subtraction.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners: understanding 2D arrays and basic arithmetic operations. A 2D array is a grid of numbers arranged in rows and columns, like a matrix. In programming, it's often represented as a list of lists: each inner list is a row. For example, [[1,2],[3,4]] has 2 rows and 2 columns: row 0 is [1,2], row 1 is [3,4]. To access an element, use grid[row][column] with 0-based indexing. Basic arithmetic: addition (+) and subtraction (-) of integers. In this problem, we'll add grid values to compute path sums. Time: O(1) for accessing an element or performing arithmetic. Space: O(m*n) to store the grid. This connects to L4 as it provides the basic data structure and operations needed. Common mistakes: Confusing rows and columns, off-by-one errors in indexing, incorrect arithmetic. Real code pattern: grid = [[1,2],[3,4]] creates a 2x2 grid. grid[0][1] gives 2. sum = grid[0][0] + grid[0][1] gives 3. Practice creating small grids and accessing elements.

examples.trace: Step 1: The grid has rows 0 and 1, columns 0 and 1. Step 2: Row 1 is [15,20]. Step 3: Column 0 is the first element: 15.
Step 1: Get value at grid[0][0] = 3. Step 2: Get value at grid[0][1] = 4. Step 3: Add: 3 + 4 = 7.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming with 1D Array O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using a 1D array to store DP values for the current row, updating in-place as you traverse the grid. This reduces space complexity while maintaining the same time efficiency as the 2D DP approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Path Sum with 1D DP Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding minimum path sum in a grid using bottom-up DP with O(n) space
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the Minimum Path Sum problem using bottom-up dynamic programming optimized with a 1D array. The intuition is that we only need to keep track of the current row's DP values since we process the grid row by row. For each cell grid[i][j], the minimum path sum to reach it is grid[i][j] plus the minimum of the path sum from above (dp[j]) or from the left (dp[j-1]). We initialize dp[0] with grid[0][0] and fill the first row by accumulating from left. Then for each subsequent row, we update dp[0] by adding grid[i][0] to dp[0] (since we can only come from above), and for each column j>0, we update dp[j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j-1]). The algorithm processes all m×n cells exactly once. Time complexity is O(m×n) as we visit each cell once. Space complexity is O(n) where n is the number of columns, as we maintain only one row of DP values. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete row processing logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the first element properly, incorrectly handling the first row/column edge cases, or using the wrong comparison for min(). The key insight is that we can overwrite dp array in-place as we move right through each row.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Process Single Row with 1D DP Update
ladders.desc: Update DP array for one row given previous row's DP values and current row's grid values
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine processes a single row of the grid to update the DP array. Given the current DP array (representing minimum path sums to the previous row) and the current row's grid values, we compute new DP values for reaching each cell in the current row. The intuition is that for column 0, we can only come from above (dp[0] + grid[i][0]). For other columns j>0, we can come from above (dp[j]) or from the left (dp[j-1]), so we take the minimum of these two plus grid[i][j]. We update the DP array in-place from left to right. The algorithm: 1. Update dp[0] += grid[i][0]. 2. For j from 1 to n-1: dp[j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j-1]). Time complexity is O(n) for processing one row. Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the DP array. This connects to L0 as the core row-processing loop. Common mistakes include updating dp[j] before using dp[j-1] (should be fine since we process left to right), or incorrectly handling the first column. The key is that we're reusing the same dp array, overwriting old values as we compute new ones.

examples.trace: Start with dp = [1,4,5]. Update dp[0] = dp[0] + grid_row[0] = 1+1=2. dp now [2,4,5]. For j=1: dp[1] = grid_row[1] + min(dp[1], dp[0]) = 5+min(4,2)=5+2=7. dp now [2,7,5]. For j=2: dp[2] = grid_row[2] + min(dp[2], dp[1]) = 1+min(5,7)=1+5=6. Final dp = [2,7,6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — In-Place DP Update with Minimum Comparison
ladders.desc: The central technique of updating DP values by comparing two adjacent DP cells
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic of comparing two DP values to determine the minimum path. For any cell (i,j) where j>0, the minimum path sum is grid[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j-1]), where dp[j] represents the path from above (previous row, same column) and dp[j-1] represents the path from the left (same row, previous column). The intuition is that we're always choosing the cheaper path to reach the current cell. We update dp[j] in-place with this new value. The algorithm is simply: new_dp_j = grid[i][j] + min(old_dp_j, old_dp_j_minus_1). We process from left to right so that when we compute dp[j], dp[j-1] has already been updated for the current row, but dp[j] still holds the value from the previous row. Time complexity is O(1) per cell. Space complexity is O(1) additional. This connects to L1 as the inner loop operation. Common mistakes include comparing the wrong indices or not adding grid[i][j]. The key insight is that min(dp[j], dp[j-1]) gives us the minimum cost to reach the cell immediately before (i,j) in the path.

examples.trace: We compute min(dp_above, dp_left) = min(4,2) = 2. Then add grid_val: 5 + 2 = 7. So the new dp value for this cell is 7.
min(1,6) = 1. 3 + 1 = 4. New dp value is 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Initialize First Row of DP Array
ladders.desc: Set up initial DP values for the first row by cumulative sum
ladders.explanation: This building block initializes the DP array for the first row of the grid. Since we can only move right in the first row (no moves from above), the minimum path sum to each cell (0,j) is the cumulative sum of grid[0][0] to grid[0][j]. The intuition is that there's only one path along the top row. The algorithm: 1. Create dp array of size n (number of columns). 2. Set dp[0] = grid[0][0]. 3. For j from 1 to n-1: dp[j] = dp[j-1] + grid[0][j]. Time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. This connects to L2 as it provides the initial state before we start processing subsequent rows with the min comparison logic. Common mistakes include starting the loop at j=0 (should start at 1) or forgetting to set dp[0]. The key is establishing the base case for the DP recurrence.

examples.trace: Create dp array of size 3. dp[0] = grid[0] = 1. For j=1: dp[1] = dp[0] + grid[1] = 1+3=4. For j=2: dp[2] = dp[1] + grid[2] = 4+1=5. Final dp = [1,4,5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Cumulative Sum of Array
ladders.desc: Compute running total of array elements
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the cumulative sum of an array: each element becomes the sum of all previous elements including itself. The intuition is simple accumulation: start with the first element, then for each subsequent element, add it to the running total. The algorithm: 1. Create result array of same size as input. 2. Set result[0] = arr[0]. 3. For i from 1 to n-1: result[i] = result[i-1] + arr[i]. Time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the result array. This connects to L3 as it's exactly what we do to initialize the first row DP array. Common mistakes include starting the loop at i=0 (causes index error) or not initializing result[0]. This is a fundamental array processing pattern.

examples.trace: Create result array size 4. result[0] = 1. i=1: result[1] = result[0]+2 = 1+2=3. i=2: result[2] = result[1]+3 = 3+3=6. i=3: result[3] = result[2]+4 = 6+4=10. Final: [1,3,6,10].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Find the smaller of two numbers
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: finding the minimum of two numbers. Given two numbers a and b, we want to determine which is smaller. The intuition is straightforward comparison: if a < b, then a is the minimum; otherwise, b is the minimum (if a = b, either is fine). The algorithm is simply: if a < b: return a else: return b. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it's the core comparison operation used in the DP update logic. Common mistakes include using > instead of <, or not handling equal values correctly (they should return either value). This fundamental operation is used throughout the DP approach to choose the cheaper path.

examples.trace: Compare 3 and 5. Since 3 < 5, the minimum is 3.
Compare 7 and 2. Since 7 > 2, the minimum is 2.
